segments.s008-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بَشنید is simple past 3rd-person singular 'he heard'. از ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'from them' (the speakers), سپهبد is the subject/title 'the spahbed' performing the hearing, and سخن is the thing heard 'speech/words' — the whole clause means 'when the spahbed heard words from them'. Note the flexible, poetic word order: the verb precedes some argument positions.
segments.s008-g34: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain'; نامور modifies it 'renowned'. نامه is the direct object 'letter'. افکند is past 3rd-person 'he cast/put/sent' (here transitive). بن is a noun 'base/bottom' used adverbially/locatively (literally 'at the base' or 'down below') and attaches to افکند to indicate where/with what effect the letter was cast.
segments.s008-g35: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. یادگاری is the noun 'memorial/memento/inscription' serving as the subject. شد is the past/inchoative 'became/came to be'. اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' — together: 'thus a memorial came to be in the world.'
segments.s008-g36: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him' (i.e., directed at him). آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise' (not the verb 'to create'), and از کهان و مهان gives the source or doers 'from the ancients and the guests' — idiomatically 'people of old and the visitors (or great ones)' praised him. The clause is elliptical: 'upon him was acclamation from ...' rather than a full finite clause.
segments.s008-g1: This clause contains a free relative 'هر چه گویم' ('whatever I say') where 'هر' + 'چه' forms 'whatever' and 'گویم' is a 1st-person present/subjunctive verb ('I say' / 'I may say'). The topic 'سخن' ('words/speech') sits at the start, and the result clause 'همه گفته‌اند' ('all have said') places the subject 'همه' before the perfect verb 'گفته‌اند' — a typical Persian word order (topic — subordinate clause — main clause).
segments.s008-g2: Prepositional phrase 'بر باغ دانش' ('to/upon the garden of knowledge') uses the poetic preposition 'بر' followed by the noun+modifier 'باغ دانش'. The clause-final perfect 'رفته‌اند' ('they have gone') takes the subject 'همه' immediately before it, yielding 'everyone has gone (to the garden of learning)'.
segments.s008-g3: The conditional introducer 'اگر' ('if') opens the clause. 'بر درخت برومند جای' is a noun phrase with the preposition 'بر' ('on/upon') followed by the noun 'درخت' and its adjective 'برومند' (poetic adjective 'lofty/noble'), and then 'جای' ('place') as the thing sought; Persian often allows this flexible ordering in poetry.
segments.s008-g4: 'نیابم' is the negative subjunctive/present form of 'یافتن' ('I do not find / I may not find'), completing the conditional 'اگر ... نیابم' ('if I do not/cannot find'). 'که' introduces a reason ('for/because'), and the phrase 'از بر شدن' uses the preposition 'از' plus the infinitive/gerundial idea 'بر شدن' ('getting on, mounting') followed by the copular negative 'نیست' and the noun 'رای' ('counsel/plan') — 'there is no counsel/way for mounting it.'
segments.s008-g5: 'کسی کو شود' is an archaic/poetic structure where 'کسی' ('anyone') is followed by the relative/archaic particle 'کو' (functioning like 'who/that') and the verb 'شود' ('becomes/happens to be'). The location phrase 'زیر نخل بلند' ('beneath a tall palm') follows, giving the meaning 'anyone who is beneath a tall palm.'
segments.s008-g6: Subject 'همان سایه' ('that very shade') is followed by the verb 'بازدارد' (3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of 'بازداشتن' meaning 'restrain/ward off') and the object 'گزند' ('harm'). The prepositional/agent-like 'ز او' ('from/of it / from him') indicates from whom/what the harm is kept away — here 'او' refers back to the person under the palm or the tree, so the clause reads 'that shade will keep harm away from him.'
segments.s008-g7: 'توانم' is the 1st-person present ('I can') and is followed by 'مگر' used here to limit the speaker's power ('except/only'), introducing the infinitival object 'پایه‌ای ساختن' ('to build a small base/footing'). The verb 'ساختن' is in its infinitive form functioning as a verbal noun (object of 'توانم').
segments.s008-g8: The purpose/location phrase 'بر شاخ آن سرو' ('upon the bough/branch of that cypress') uses 'بر' + noun phrase, then the imperative/optative 'سایه فکن' ('put/throw a shade') where 'فکن' is an imperative of 'فکندن' (poetic 'set/cast'). Together the segment gives the intended action/result of the small foundation: setting a shade on the cypress bough.
segments.s008-g25: بپرسیدشان is an imperative verb meaning “ask them” (second-person plural/polite). از introduces the topic here (= “about/concerning”); کیان refers to the Kayanian kings (a proper collective name) and جهان follows as the domain ‘the world’ — together “the kings of the world.” Word order is topic marker (از + noun) after the verb, typical of Persian poetic word order.
segments.s008-g26: وزان is a conjunctive prepositional phrase meaning “and from/and of those,” linking the next phrase to the previous question. نامداران is an adjectival plural meaning “renowned/illustrious,” فرخ is an adjective “fortunate/auspicious,” and مهان here denotes notable persons/lords (literally ‘guests/visitors’ in the lexical list but used honorifically). The three words form a noun phrase “those famed, fortunate lords.”
segments.s008-g27: که introduces a subordinate clause “how/that.” گیتی means “the world,” and به آغاز (“at the beginning”) gives the temporal frame. چون here means “how,” and داشتند is the past-tense verb “they had/held” (so the clause asks about the state of the world at its origin). The word order places the temporal phrase before the question word + past verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s008-g28: The second that-clause continues the account: که (that/when) + ایدون (an adverb/verb-form here indicating the present/time that has come) + به ما (“to/for us”) + خوار (“low/despised”) + بگذاشتند (they left/placed). Together it states that now they have left us in a low/despised state; بگذاشتند is a past transitive verb “they left/caused” used here with a resultative sense.
segments.s008-g29: چگونه = “how.” سر آمد is an idiomatic verbal expression meaning “came to pass / came upon / ended” (سر + آمد together form the verbal idea). به + نیک اختری literally “under/by a good/auspicious star” is an ablative/agentive phrase describing the heavenly circumstance under which the event occurred (نیک here is the adjective “auspicious,” and اختری is a star-related adjective/noun).
segments.s008-g30: بر ایشان = “upon them.” همه روز = “every day” (همه used distributively). کندآوری is a deverbal noun/compound (from کندآور) meaning “bringing slowness/affliction” or more generally “causing hardship/penalty” — here it describes a recurring misfortune that each day inflicts on them. The phrase as a whole: “each day brought (or inflicted) misery upon them.”
segments.s008-g31: بگفتند is a past-tense verb meaning “they said/they told.” پیشش here means “in his presence / before him.” یکایک = “one by one” or “each in turn,” and مهان again refers to the notable persons/lords (lexically ‘guests/visitors’). The phrase describes the guests telling things individually in the king’s presence.
segments.s008-g32: سخن‌های is the plural construct “words/tales/speeches” (here: accounts or stories). شاهان = “kings.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” گشت (from گشتن) here is nominal/verb sense “the turning/happening” (the events or course of) and جهان = “the world,” so the phrase means “the tales of the kings and the happenings/turnings of the world.”
segments.s008-g9: کز is the literary contraction of که از meaning 'from/that from'; it introduces source. این نامور نامه شهریار is a noun phrase in which این ('this/the foregoing') modifies نامور ('renowned') and نامه is used in the sense of a book/treatise rather than a short letter; شهریار ('the sovereign/king') is the possessor (book of the king). Word order is demonstrative + adjective + noun + possessor (possessive expressed by proximity rather than an explicit genitive particle).
segments.s008-g10: به گیتی بمانم یکی یادگار: به here has a directional/locative force ('into/within' the world). بمانم is first-person singular of ماندن in a present/subjunctive/optative form often used in poetry — here rendered as a declarative 'I remain / I shall remain'. یکی یادگار is an indefinite nominal phrase 'one memorial/a single memento' functioning as predicate. The sequence places the locative phrase before the verb, as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s008-g11: تو این را دروغ و فسانه مدان: تو is the vocative/2nd-person ('thou/you' in poetic address). این را uses را as the definite object marker ('this' as direct object). دروغ و فسانه are coordinated nouns 'lie and fable'. مدان is an archaic negative imperative (classical form of 'مَدان') meaning 'do not consider/regard' — here it negates the whole object phrase.
segments.s008-g12: به رنگ فسون و بهانه مدان: به here functions adverbially ('by/in the guise of' or 'as'), and رنگ is used figuratively as 'appearance/guise'. فسون means 'enchantment' and بهانه 'pretext/excuse'; the coordinating و links the two readings. The repeated مدان again gives the negative imperative 'do not take/regard (it) as ...', so the clause restates the prohibition with two alternative characterizations.
segments.s008-g13: از او هر چه اندر خورد با خرد: از او here means 'of/about him' or 'that concerned him'; هر چه is the indefinite 'whatever/whatever thing' and اندر خورد is a poetic/periphrastic past meaning 'befell/ happened' (خورد is here 'to befall' rather than 'to eat'). با خرد is an instrumental phrase 'with wisdom' modifying the action and is placed after the verb phrase, as is common in Persian.
segments.s008-g14: دگر بر ره رمز و معنی برد: دگر here means 'another/otherwise' or 'into another form' (i.e. 'he turned/converted them'). بر ره is a locative phrase 'onto the path/way'. رمز و معنی are coordinated nouns 'secret/hidden sense and meaning' (i.e. allegorical sense). برد in classical diction is the past 3rd-person singular of بردن 'he carried/took/led', but the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense; grammatically this is a past-tense verb completing the clause.
segments.s008-g15: یکی نامه بود از گه باستان: یکی is an indefinite pre‑modifier meaning 'a certain' or 'one (a)'. نامه here again means 'book/treatise'. بود is the past copula 'there was/it was'. از گه باستان is a temporal/genitive phrase 'from the time/age of antiquity' (گه باستان = ancient time). The order is typical: indefinite + noun + past copula + temporal/genitive modifier.
segments.s008-g16: فراوان بدو اندرون داستان: فراوان is an adverb/adjective meaning 'abundant/plentiful' modifying the following nouns. بدو is the clitic pronoun 'to it/unto it' (here 'in it' or 'within it'). اندرون is 'inside/within' (reinforcing interior location) and داستان means 'stories/tales'. The phrase thus says 'within it (there were) many tales' — the copula 'was/were' is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s008-g17: پراگنده is an adjective/participial descriptor meaning 'scattered/widely spread' placed before the locative phrase that follows. در دست هر موبدی is a locative prepositional phrase ('in the hand(s) of each mobad') with the preposition در + noun دست and the quantifier هر modifying موبدی.
segments.s008-g18: از او بهره‌ای is a verbless clause-like phrase meaning 'a share/portion from it' (از = 'from', او = 'it/he', بهره‌ای = 'a portion'). نزد هر بخردی follows as a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/in the presence/opinion of every wise one' (نزد functioning like 'with/to/among'). Together they complete the idea begun in the previous segment.
segments.s008-g19: یکی پهلوان بود دهقان‌نژاد is a nominal clause meaning 'there was a certain champion of peasant-stock.' یکی here functions as 'a certain' (indefinite), پهلوان is the noun, بود is the past copula 'was', and دهقان‌نژاد is a post-nominal adjective/compound describing origin ('of peasant birth').
segments.s008-g20: دلیر و بزرگ و خردمند و راد is a string of coordinate adjectives joined by و ('and'), all modifying the preceding noun (the champion). In Persian adjective order is flexible; here adjectives follow the noun and are simply listed for emphasis.
segments.s008-g21: پژوهنده روزگار نخست is a noun phrase meaning 'one who investigates the earliest times.' پژوهنده is an agent noun ('researcher/inquirer') and روزگار نخست is a genitival/nominal phrase ('the first/earliest times') that the agent investigates; the head (پژوهنده) comes first in the line.
segments.s008-g22: گذشته سخن‌ها همه باز جست is the verbal clause: گذشته سخن‌ها 'the past tales' (object/topic) + همه 'all' + باز (adverb 'again' or 'back') + جست (past tense of جستن 'to seek/search'). The verb جست is past tense and takes the preceding noun phrase as its object: 'he searched/searched back for all the ancient tales.'
segments.s008-g23: ز هر کشوری موبدی سال‌خورد is a noun phrase giving origin and description: ز هر کشوری 'from every country/land' (ز = poetic 'from'), followed by موبدی سال‌خورد 'an aged mobad' (موبدی = priest, سال‌خورد = 'aged/time-worn' postpositive adjective). The prepositional phrase sets the source and the adjective follows the noun.
segments.s008-g24: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought/caused to come.' It is followed by a subordinate (relative) clause introduced by ک(ه) (here written کاین): کاین نامه را یاد کرد 'who/that remembered this book.' یاد کرد is a compound of یاد + کرد functioning as 'remembered' or 'recalled' and را marks نامه as the definite object.

sentences.s008-s17: When the spahbed heard their words, he cast down a certain renowned letter at the base.
sentences.s008-s18: Thus a memorial came to be in the world; upon him was acclamation from the ancients and the guests.
sentences.s008-s1: Whatever words I may speak have all already been said; everyone has gone to the garden of learning.
sentences.s008-s2: If I cannot find a place upon the lofty tree — for there is no counsel for getting up on it.
sentences.s008-s3: If anyone happens to be beneath a tall palm, that very shade will ward off harm from him.
sentences.s008-s4: All I can do is build a small foundation and place a shade upon the bough of that cypress.
sentences.s008-s13: Ask them about the Kayanian kings of the world and about those famed, fortunate lords.
sentences.s008-s14: How the world was at the beginning, and how (later) they left us despised.
sentences.s008-s15: How an end came (to pass) under a fortunate star, and how each day brought affliction upon them.
sentences.s008-s16: They recited before him, one by one, the accounts of the lords and the turning of the world.
sentences.s008-s5: By this famed book of the king I remain in the world as a memorial.
sentences.s008-s6: Do not regard this as a lie and a fable; do not call it the guise of enchantment or a mere pretext.
sentences.s008-s7: Whatever of it befell him, he with wisdom turned (or carried) the rest onto the path of symbol and meaning.
sentences.s008-s8: There was a certain book from ancient times; within it were many tales.
sentences.s008-s9: It was widely circulated in the hands of every mobad; a portion of it was with every wise man.
sentences.s008-s10: There was a champion of peasant birth — brave, great, wise, and generous.
sentences.s008-s11: A seeker of the earliest times searched through the past; he sought all the old tales.
sentences.s008-s12: From every country he brought an aged mobad who remembered this book.
